questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: MOTION IN ONE AND TWO DIMENSIONS
questions.question: What is the SI unit of angular displacement?
questions.hint: Angular displacement is measured in dimensionless units.
questions.explanation: Angular displacement \(\theta = s/r\) is the ratio of arc length to radius, making it dimensionless; the SI unit is the radian. Degrees and revolutions are also units but not SI.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: MOTION IN ONE AND TWO DIMENSIONS
source_trace.content_anchor: The unit of angular displacement in the SI system is radian

explanations_tiered.definition: Angular displacement is the angle through which an object rotates, measured in radians.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Radian is the SI unit because \(\theta = s/r\) gives a pure number; one radian is the angle subtended by an arc equal to the radius.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Angles in physics are measured in radians, not degrees, for rotation problems.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Angular displacement \(\theta\) has SI unit radian, where \(1 \text{ rad} = \text{arc length} / \text{radius}\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Radian is dimensionless but is a coherent derived unit in SI; conversions: \(2\pi \text{ rad} = 360^\circ\).
reasoning_steps: s and r both in meters → units cancel → radian.
misconceptions: Degree is common but not SI for calculations.
Revolutions are used but must convert to radians.

publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize radian over degree in circular motion equations.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Radian is the SI unit for angular displacement.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: MOTION IN ONE AND TWO DIMENSIONS
questions.question: A wheel of radius \(0.5 \ \mathrm{m}\) rotates through an angular displacement of \(4.0 \ \mathrm{rad}\). What is the tangential displacement of a point on the rim?
questions.explanation: Tangential displacement \(s = r\theta = 0.5 \times 4.0 = 2.0 \ \mathrm{m}\). Option C is the angular displacement value, not multiplied by radius.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: MOTION IN ONE AND TWO DIMENSIONS

explanations_tiered.definition: Tangential displacement is the arc length \(s = r\theta\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: A point on a rotating object moves a distance s along the circle equal to radius times angular displacement in radians.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If a wheel turns, a point on the edge travels a distance = radius \u00d7 angle turned (in radians).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Angular displacement must be in radians for \(s = r\theta\) to hold; it arises from the definition \(\theta = s/r\).
misconceptions: Using \(\theta\) in degrees gives wrong length.
Forgetting to multiply by radius gives \(\theta\) itself as displacement (option C).

glossary_entry.definition: Arc length traveled along circle: \(s = r\theta\).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: MOTION IN ONE AND TWO DIMENSIONS
questions.question: What is the definition of angular velocity?

options.A: Rate of change of angular displacement
options.B: Rate of change of tangential velocity

questions.explanation: Angular velocity \(\omega\) is defined as the rate of change of angular displacement: \(\omega = \Delta\theta/\Delta t\). Option C describes tangential velocity, not angular.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: MOTION IN ONE AND TWO DIMENSIONS
source_trace.content_anchor: Angular velocity measures the rate at which angular displacement changes

explanations_tiered.definition: Angular velocity is the time rate of change of angular displacement.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: \(\omega\) tells how fast an object rotates, measured in radians per second.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Angular velocity is how many radians per second an object spins.
reasoning_steps: It's analogous to linear velocity \(v = \Delta x/\Delta t\).
misconceptions: Confusing \(\omega\) with tangential speed \(v_t = \omega r\).
Using degrees per second is not SI but possible; rad/s is standard.

glossary_entry.definition: Rate of change of angular displacement, \(\omega = \Delta\theta/\Delta t\).

flashcard.back: Rate of change of angular displacement (\u03c9 = \u0394\u03b8/\u0394t).

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Rate of change of angular displacement.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: MOTION IN ONE AND TWO DIMENSIONS
questions.question: What is the relationship between tangential velocity \(v_t\) and angular velocity \(\omega\) for a point at radius \(r\)?
questions.explanation: Tangential speed \(v_t = s/t = (r\theta)/t = r(\theta/t) = \omega r\). Option A inverts the relationship.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: MOTION IN ONE AND TWO DIMENSIONS
source_trace.structural_element: Relationship between angular and tangential velocity

explanations_tiered.definition: Tangential speed equals angular speed times radius: \(v_t = \omega r\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Points farther from the axis have larger tangential speeds even if \(\omega\) is the same.
explanations_tiered.beginner: A point on the edge of a merry-go-round moves faster than a point near the center when it spins.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Derivative of \(s = r\theta\) gives \(ds/dt = r d\theta/dt\) → \(v_t = \omega r\), assuming r constant.
misconceptions: Thinking \(v_t = \omega/r\) (larger radius gives slower speed) is wrong.
Confusing \(v_t\) with \(a_c = v_t^2/r\).

flashcard.front: v_t in terms of \u03c9 and r?

publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Important for relating linear and rotational motion.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: MOTION IN ONE AND TWO DIMENSIONS
questions.question: A wheel of radius \(0.4 \ \mathrm{m}\) rotates with angular velocity \(15 \ \mathrm{rad/s}\). What is the tangential speed of a point on the rim?
questions.explanation: Tangential speed \(v_t = \omega r = 15 \times 0.4 = 6.0 \ \mathrm{m/s}\). Option A is \(r/\omega\), option D is \(\omega^2 r\) or multiplication error.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: MOTION IN ONE AND TWO DIMENSIONS
source_trace.structural_element: Relationship between angular and tangential velocity

explanations_tiered.beginner: If a wheel spins fast (big \u03c9) and is large (big r), the edge speed is big.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Derived from \(v_t = r \omega\), a direct proportionality.
misconceptions: Using \(v_t = \omega / r\) gives 37.5 (option D reversed?). Actually 15/0.4 = 37.5, option D.
Forgetting to convert units if needed.

glossary_entry.definition: Linear speed of a point on a rotating object: v_t = \u03c9 r.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: MOTION IN ONE AND TWO DIMENSIONS
questions.question: What is the direction of centripetal acceleration in uniform circular motion?
questions.explanation: Centripetal acceleration is always directed toward the center of the circular path, perpendicular to the velocity. Option B would be centrifugal, not centripetal.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: MOTION IN ONE AND TWO DIMENSIONS
source_trace.content_anchor: direction is always towards the center of the circle

explanations_tiered.definition: Centripetal acceleration is the acceleration toward the center that causes circular motion.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Even with constant speed, the direction changes, requiring acceleration toward the center.
explanations_tiered.beginner: When you swing a ball on a string, the string pulls it toward the center; that's the direction of acceleration.
explanations_tiered.advanced: From \(\vec{a}_c = -\frac{v^2}{r} \hat{r}\), the negative radial unit vector indicates toward center.
reasoning_steps: Acceleration is always perpendicular to velocity, pointing inward.
misconceptions: Centrifugal (outward) is a fictitious force in rotating frame, not real acceleration in inertial frame.
Tangential acceleration is different; centripetal is radial.

glossary_entry.definition: Acceleration toward center of circle, magnitude v\u00b2/r.

flashcard.back: Toward the center of the circle.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is the direction of centripetal acceleration?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Toward the center of the circular path.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Centripetal means toward center.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: MOTION IN ONE AND TWO DIMENSIONS
questions.question: Which formula correctly gives centripetal acceleration in terms of angular velocity \(\omega\) and radius \(r\)?
questions.explanation: \(a_c = v^2/r = (\omega r)^2/r = \omega^2 r\). Option A is tangential velocity, not acceleration.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: MOTION IN ONE AND TWO DIMENSIONS

explanations_tiered.definition: Centripetal acceleration can be expressed as \(a_c = \omega^2 r\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: For a spinning object, centripetal acceleration gets bigger if it spins faster (big \u03c9) or has larger radius.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Substituting \(v = \omega r\) into \(a_c = v^2/r\) gives \(a_c = \omega^2 r\).
misconceptions: Using \(a_c = \omega r\) (that's v_t).
Using \(a_c = \omega r^2\) has wrong units.

flashcard.front: a_c in terms of \u03c9 and r?

publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Derive from v = \u03c9r and a_c = v\u00b2/r.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: MOTION IN ONE AND TWO DIMENSIONS
questions.question: A stone moves in a circle of radius \(2.0 \ \mathrm{m}\) with tangential speed \(6.0 \ \mathrm{m/s}\). What is the centripetal acceleration?
questions.explanation: \(a_c = v^2/r = (6.0)^2 / 2.0 = 36 / 2 = 18 \ \mathrm{m/s^2}\). Option D is v\u00b2 without dividing by r.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: MOTION IN ONE AND TWO DIMENSIONS

explanations_tiered.definition: Centripetal acceleration equals tangential speed squared divided by radius.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: For a given speed, smaller radius gives larger centripetal acceleration.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you swing a stone faster or in a smaller circle, the pull toward the center is stronger.
misconceptions: Forgetting to square v gives 6/2=3 (option A).
Using v\u00b2 only gives 36 (option D).

flashcard.front: v=6 m/s, r=2 m, find a_c.

publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Common error: forgetting to square v.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: MOTION IN ONE AND TWO DIMENSIONS
questions.question: Why does an object moving in uniform circular motion have acceleration even if its speed is constant?
questions.hint: Acceleration is change in velocity, which is a vector.
questions.explanation: Velocity is a vector; changing direction changes velocity even if speed is constant. That change requires acceleration (centripetal). Option B is false for uniform circular motion.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: MOTION IN ONE AND TWO DIMENSIONS
source_trace.content_anchor: an object undergoing uniform circular motion is always accelerating, because it always changes its direction of motion

explanations_tiered.definition: Acceleration occurs when velocity changes in magnitude or direction.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: In uniform circular motion, direction constantly changes, so velocity changes, giving centripetal acceleration.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Even if you run around a track at steady speed, you're turning, which is a kind of acceleration.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Velocity is a vector; changing direction means Δv ≠ 0, so a = Δv/Δt ≠ 0.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The derivative of velocity vector \(\vec{v}(t) = v \hat{\theta}(t)\) gives \(\vec{a} = -\frac{v^2}{r} \hat{r}\), nonzero.
reasoning_steps: Recall acceleration = rate of change of velocity.
Direction changes in circular motion → velocity changes → acceleration exists.
misconceptions: Thinking constant speed means zero acceleration.
Confusing speed with velocity.

glossary_entry.definition: Caused by change in velocity direction, not magnitude.

flashcard.front: Why is there acceleration if speed is constant in circular motion?
flashcard.back: Velocity changes direction, so velocity changes → acceleration.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Constant speed in circle: is there acceleration?
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Acceleration exists even if speed constant (direction change).
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Direction change means velocity changes → acceleration.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: MOTION IN ONE AND TWO DIMENSIONS
questions.question: A student says: 'If a car rounds a curve at constant speed, there is no acceleration because the speedometer reading doesn't change.' What is the flaw?

options.B: Acceleration requires a change in speed
options.C: Direction change causes acceleration even with constant speed
options.D: The car is actually slowing down

questions.hint: Acceleration is change in velocity, not just change in speed.
questions.explanation: The student confuses speed with velocity. Rounding a curve changes direction, so velocity changes, producing centripetal acceleration even if speed is constant. Option B is incorrect; acceleration can occur without speed change (direction change).

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: MOTION IN ONE AND TWO DIMENSIONS
source_trace.content_anchor: object undergoing uniform circular motion is always accelerating, because it always changes its direction of motion

explanations_tiered.definition: Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity, a vector; direction change implies acceleration.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: A car at constant speed on a curve has centripetal acceleration because its direction changes.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Turning a corner at steady speed still feels like a pull to the side \u2013 that's acceleration.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Velocity vector changes direction on a curve, so Δv ≠ 0, giving acceleration (centripetal).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Even if \(dv/dt = 0\) (speed constant), the derivative of direction unit vector gives \(a = v^2/r\) inward.
misconceptions: Believing constant speed → zero acceleration.
Confusing speedometer (speed) with velocity.

glossary_entry.definition: Centripetal acceleration due to direction change.

flashcard.front: Constant speed on a curve: acceleration?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Car at constant speed on a curve: no acceleration?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: No: direction change gives centripetal acceleration.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Acceleration can occur without speed change if direction changes.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Curve → acceleration even if speed constant.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Direction change causes acceleration (centripetal) even with constant speed.
